annotations.label: Symmetry (mathematical)

anchor.snippet: the term has a more precise definition and is usually used to refer to an object that is invariant under some transformations

annotations.note: An informal umbrella notion; Mathlib instead formalizes specific symmetries (e.g. `Function.Involutive`, `Symmetric` relations, group actions) rather than the general concept.

annotations.label: Symmetric geometric object

anchor.snippet: A geometric shape or object is symmetric if it can be divided into two or more identical pieces that are arranged in an organized fashion

annotations.note: Informal geometric notion; no direct Mathlib formalization of `symmetric geometric object` as a primitive.

annotations.label: Reflectional symmetry

anchor.snippet: An object has reflectional symmetry (line or mirror symmetry) if there is a line

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `EuclideanGeometry.reflection` and `Module.reflection`, but the predicate `has reflectional symmetry` of an object/set is not directly defined.

annotations.label: Rotational symmetry

anchor.snippet: An object has rotational symmetry if the object can be rotated about a fixed point

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Rotation` in oriented Euclidean geometry but no predicate stating an object has rotational symmetry.

annotations.label: Translational symmetry

anchor.snippet: An object has translational symmetry if it can be translated

annotations.note: Translations are encoded via `AddAction`/`vadd`, but `translational symmetry` of an object is not a defined predicate.

annotations.label: Helical symmetry

anchor.snippet: An object has helical symmetry if it can be simultaneously translated and rotated

annotations.note: No formalization of helical/screw symmetry was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Scale symmetry

anchor.snippet: An object has scale symmetry if it does not change shape when it is expanded or contracted

annotations.note: No definition of scale/dilation symmetry of an object found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Glide reflection and rotoreflection symmetry

anchor.snippet: Other symmetries include glide reflection symmetry (a reflection followed by a translation) and rotoreflection symmetry

annotations.note: Glide reflections and rotoreflections are not specifically defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Symmetric dyadic relation

anchor.snippet: A dyadic relation R = S × S is symmetric if for all elements a , b in S , whenever it is true that Rab , it is also true that Rba

annotations.note: `Symmetric r := ∀ ⦃x y⦄, r x y → r y x` and the bundled `IsSymm` class match the standard definition exactly.

annotations.label: Symmetric logical connectives

anchor.snippet: symmetric binary logical connectives include and (∧, or &), or (∨, or |) and if and only if (↔), while the connective if (→) is not symmetric

annotations.note: Symmetry of `∧`, `∨`, `↔` is captured by core lemmas `And.symm`, `Or.symm`, `Iff.symm` available through Mathlib.

annotations.label: Symmetric mathematical object

anchor.snippet: a mathematical object is symmetric with respect to a given mathematical operation , if, when applied to the object, this operation preserves some property of the object

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes invariance under group actions via `MulAction.stabilizer` and `FixedPoints`, but no single decl named `symmetric mathematical object`.

annotations.label: Property-preserving operations form a group

anchor.snippet: The set of operations that preserve a given property of the object form a group

annotations.note: Mathlib has stabilizers (`MulAction.stabilizer` is a subgroup) and automorphism groups, capturing this statement in standard contexts.

annotations.label: Symmetry in physics as invariance

anchor.snippet: Symmetry in physics has been generalized to mean invariance —that is, lack of change—under any kind of transformation

annotations.note: A physics meta-definition not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Noether's theorem

anchor.snippet: for every continuous mathematical symmetry, there is a corresponding conserved quantity such as energy or momentum

annotations.note: Noether's theorem (the physics version about conserved quantities) is not in Mathlib; the `Noether` names found refer to Noetherian rings/spaces.

annotations.label: Wigner's classification

anchor.snippet: Wigner's classification , which says that the symmetries of the laws of physics determine the properties of the particles found in nature

annotations.note: Grep for `Wigner` in Mathlib returned no files; not formalized.

annotations.label: Symmetry operation (chemistry)

anchor.snippet: A symmetry operation is an action that moves a molecule in such a way that its overall appearance remains unchanged

annotations.note: Chemistry-specific notion of symmetry operation has no formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: n-fold axis of symmetry

anchor.snippet: This axis is commonly denoted as C n , where n indicates the number of times the molecule matches its original appearance during a full 360° rotation

annotations.note: Schoenflies `Cₙ` rotation axes are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Identity operation E

anchor.snippet: the act of making no change at all is called the identity operation, and it is represented by the symbol E

annotations.note: The identity map exists generally as `id`/`1` in groups, but the chemistry-specific identity operation `E` is not a named decl.

annotations.label: n-fold rotation relation

anchor.snippet: performing the rotation n times brings the molecule back to its original orientation

annotations.note: No Mathlib lemma states the order-`n` property of a chemistry `Cₙ` axis.

annotations.label: Plane of symmetry

anchor.snippet: There are different types of mirror planes, depending on their orientation relative to the molecule

annotations.note: Chemistry mirror planes (`σ_h`, `σ_v`, `σ_d`) are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Reflection squared equals identity

anchor.snippet: If a reflection operation is applied twice, the molecule returns to its original position

annotations.note: `Module.reflection` (and `EuclideanGeometry.reflection`) are proved involutive, capturing that reflection squared is the identity.

annotations.label: Inversion through a center

anchor.snippet: Inversion can be thought of as a combination of a 180° rotation followed by a reflection through a plane perpendicular to the rotation axis

annotations.note: Mathlib has `EuclideanGeometry.inversion` (the conformal/Möbius inversion) and point reflection via `Equiv.pointReflection`; the latter formalizes the chemistry concept.

annotations.label: Inversion squared equals identity

anchor.snippet: Applying the inversion operation twice returns the molecule to its original configuration

annotations.note: `Equiv.pointReflection` is involutive, so point inversion squared is the identity.
